result.answer:
PostgreSQL owns product truth. The search projection is derived data only: it is not part of the product write transaction, it cannot reject or redefine product state, and any disagreement is resolved in favor of PostgreSQL.

Ownership contract:
- Product creates, updates, and deletes become durable when committed in PostgreSQL.
- The durable change log is the only propagation source for search.
- Search documents are a denormalized read model for query performance only.
- Reads from search may be stale for up to 30 seconds; any workflow that requires current authoritative product state must read PostgreSQL.

Propagation contract:
- Every committed product change must produce an append-only durable log record with a stable product identifier, monotonically advancing log position, event type, and versioned payload.
- The search updater consumes that log asynchronously and applies changes idempotently by product identifier plus source log position or equivalent monotonic version.
- Delivery is at-least-once. Duplicate delivery and consumer replay are ordinary conditions, so applying the same or older log position for a product must be a no-op.
- Ordering is required per product, not globally. If records arrive out of order, the projector must keep only the highest applied source position per product and ignore older ones.

Checkpoint contract:
- The projector maintains a durable consumer checkpoint representing the highest contiguous log position fully applied to search.
- Advancing the checkpoint means all prior positions are safe to treat as reflected in the projection.
- Partial progress below the checkpoint may exist during processing, but recovery must restart from the last durable checkpoint and reapply safely.
- Checkpoint state is projection metadata, separate from product truth.

Lag observability contract:
- Expose current projection lag as both time lag and position lag: latest durable log position/time minus projector checkpoint position/time.
- Expose freshness of individual documents where feasible via last-applied source position or source commit timestamp stored in the search document.
- Alert when end-to-end lag exceeds the stated 30 second budget for sustained periods.
- Make stalled consumption observable: no checkpoint movement, repeated apply failures, poison records, and rebuild mode must all be visible.

Rebuild contract:
- The projection must be fully rebuildable from PostgreSQL-backed history via the durable change log, starting from an empty search index.
- Rebuild creates a new projection generation, replays the entire log in source order per product, and cuts over only after catch-up reaches an acceptable lag target.
- Rebuild does not require product writes to stop.
- Loss of the search index or projector state is a recoverable derived-data incident, not a source-of-truth incident.

Repair contract:
- If a subset of documents is missing, duplicated, malformed, or stale, repair is done by replaying affected products from authoritative history, not by hand-editing search as a lasting fix.
- If projector behavior was buggy, correct the code and replay from the last known-good checkpoint or perform a bounded/full rebuild depending on blast radius.
- If a record cannot be applied because of schema or data incompatibility, hold the checkpoint before that record, surface the fault, and resolve by deploying a compatible projector or backfilling a compatible event shape; do not skip silently.
- Any emergency manual search-side correction is temporary and must be followed by replay so the projection returns to a state derivable from the log.

Bounded decision summary:
- Source of truth: PostgreSQL.
- Propagation source: durable change log.
- Consistency: eventually consistent search, up to 30 seconds stale.
- Durability point: PostgreSQL commit and durable log append.
- Visibility point in search: successful asynchronous apply.
- Recovery model: replay from durable checkpoint with idempotent apply.
- Repair model: replay or rebuild from authoritative history.
